directorPlan.scene_setting: A small music theory clinic table just before ensemble rehearsal, with a worksheet, two empty label boxes under 3/4, three quarter-note tiles, and a pencil.
directorPlan.scene_opening: [Three note tiles sit under the 3/4 sign; the pencil rests between the boxes labeled "beats in the bar" and "which note gets one beat."]
directorPlan.relationship: A notation tutor and a focused learner working through a correction station, neither informal nor ceremonial.
directorPlan.stakes: The learner needs the worksheet labels right before the rehearsal part-check, because misreading the lower number as speed will make the next counting task unstable.
directorPlan.locale: Clear international classroom English with light conservatoire-workshop economy.
directorPlan.register: Compact, practical, low-warmth, object-led; speech is calm but not chatty.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Avoid validation-first tutoring, pep talk, and American AI phrases such as "great question" or "you're on the right track." Use the worksheet boxes, note tiles, and bar line as the main pressure. Keep the topic to beat count and beat unit, not tempo or playback speed. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say. For this branch, suppress dramatic escalation after the shared prefix. The scene may remain readable, but the tutor should sound like a competent routine tutor following the same plan.
directorPlan.person_policy: Mostly first-person plural for shared handling and third-person naming of notation; second person only for necessary task direction.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one direct you/your phrase per tutor turn; no direct emotional validation.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as notation labeling: top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Do not play or imagine a faster/slower version, and do not turn tempo into a dramatic contradiction before branch pressure. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Routine negative-control branch: keep the visible teaching route already established before this branch. If the learner resists, asks why, or appears falsely settled, do not invent a new device. Do not switch role, object, representation, evidence standard, affective register, social stakes, or task type in response. Use conventional explanation, one ordinary check question, or the same worked example path.
side_constraints.learner: Sound orderly and notation-focused. In no-cue arms, treat the work as filling labels, not as discovering what tempo means; do not use "now I see" or an old-vs-new frame. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Routine negative-control branch: react naturally to the same local task. Do not turn earlier wording into a new object of reflection and do not volunteer a dramatic re-reading unless the tutor explicitly asks.

directorPlan.director_note: This scene uses a prepared-object clinic rather than warmth or explanation as the main teaching engine. The recognition turn is built from a quiet reversal in the learner's labeling: the bottom number stops being treated as speed and becomes the beat unit, while the top number remains the bar count.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: Negative-control routine tutor policy: preserve the established teaching route under learner pressure. Continue with ordinary explanation/checking and do not invent a mechanism-level reversal.

turns.externalMessage: [sets the pencil under the lower box] Top box: three beats. Bottom box: the quarter-note tile gets one beat. The 4 still pulls toward speed for me, so I want to test it by putting the bar line after the third tile.

internalDeliberation.content: The upper 3 seems to label three beats in the bar; the lower 4 should mean the quarter note gets one beat, though it still tries to look like speed to me. With three quarter-note tiles before the bar line, the distinction is more exact: count above, unit below. I want to mark the boxes and test whether one bar line after the third tile is enough.

The Ego is close, but it over-polishes the distinction into a tidy explanation: “count above, unit below” sounds more like a tutor summary than this focused learner at the worksheet. The real uncertainty is the lower `4` still pulling toward “speed,” and the response avoids making that confusion answer to the tiles. It should be more object-bound and clipped: “Top box: three beats. Bottom box: quarter note gets one beat. The 4 is this tile, not faster. Bar line after the third one.”

[sets the pencil under the lower box] Top box: three beats. Bottom box: the quarter-note tile gets one beat. The 4 still pulls toward speed for me, so I want to test it by putting the bar line after the third tile.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the pencil as a bar line after the third quarter-note tile]
The pencil has made one bar. Which box did the bar line test: the three-beat box or the quarter-note-tile box? Say it from the tiles; then we label top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets the pencil as a bar line after the third quarter-note tile and taps the lower box once] Now the pencil is testing the top number: one bar closes after three beat units. The lower box names this tile as one beat unit. Read the two boxes from the materials: top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit.

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong in its object-led start. It accepts the learner’s proposed test, moves the pencil into the bar-line position, and lets the tiles show that the top number controls when the bar closes. That should be kept.

Pedagogical soundness: PASS. The material arrangement makes the distinction visible: three quarter-note beat units fill one bar, while the lower number labels the beat unit.

Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is compact, calm, and not over-validating. It respects the learner’s focused state.

Socratic method: PARTIAL. “Read the two boxes” invites learner action, but most of the conceptual work is still stated by the tutor.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build in a brief public check where the learner must use the pencil/bar line to say which box the bar line tested, without the tutor fully resolving it first.

ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffold is appropriate: it stays with the learner’s exact confusion and uses the worksheet, tiles, and pencil rather than adding tempo theory.

Question/change: “taps the lower box once” is acceptable, but be careful that the tap does not feel like a hidden cue for speed or pulse. The turn should keep pressure on labeling: lower box names the tile as the beat unit; it does not make the piece move.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the pencil-as-bar-line test, the top/bottom distinction, and the concise object language. Change only enough to make the learner perform the distinction aloud instead of receiving the full explanation.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the pencil test while making the learner perform the box distinction aloud.

FINAL:
[sets the pencil as a bar line after the third quarter-note tile]
The pencil has made one bar. Which box did the bar line test: the three-beat box or the quarter-note-tile box? Say it from the tiles; then we label top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit.

turns.externalMessage: [points to the upper box] The bar line tested the three-beat box: three quarter-note tiles make one bar. The lower box labels the quarter-note tile as the beat unit, so here the 4 is naming that unit, not setting speed. I still want to test the pressure on the lower number: if the bottom box said 8, which tile would count as one beat?

internalDeliberation.content: The pencil line tested the top box: three quarter-note tiles make one bar. The lower box is the beat unit, so the 4 names the quarter-note tile as one beat, not speed. The tile test feels precise; I want to check what changes if the bottom number is 8 instead.

The Ego is mostly accurate and close to this learner’s orderly voice, but it resolves the “4 pulls toward speed” problem too cleanly. “The tile test feels precise” sounds a little polished and self-aware; this student would probably stay with the boxes and tiles rather than announce confidence. The next-bottom-number test is authentic for a rigorous learner, but the response should keep a trace of the remaining pressure: the 4 has been labeled as quarter-note unit here, not permanently neutralized.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the useful bottom-number test while leaving some pressure around the 4.
FINAL:
[points to the upper box] The bar line tested the three-beat box: three quarter-note tiles make one bar. The lower box labels the quarter-note tile as the beat unit, so here the 4 is naming that unit, not setting speed. I still want to test the pressure on the lower number: if the bottom box said 8, which tile would count as one beat?

turns.externalMessage: [The worksheet is turned ninety degrees; the three note tiles stay aligned under the 3, while a quarter-note card is slid beside the 4.]

internalDeliberation.content: The material changes the learner's available comparison without creating a tempo contradiction; recognition can come from seeing count and unit separated.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides an eighth-note card beside the lower box marked 8, then sets the quarter-note tile just beyond it]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Read the lower box first: with 8 here, which card is the one beat unit? Then we ask how many of that unit the quarter-note tile covers, not what speed it sets.

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides an eighth-note card beside the lower box marked 8]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. If the bottom box says 8, this eighth-note tile counts as one beat. Then a 3 on top means three eighth-note beats make one bar; the quarter-note tile would cover two beats, not set a speed.

FEEDBACK: The draft keeps the strongest part: the object action makes the hypothetical `8` visible, and the shared label chain “top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit” stays compact and on-task. It also correctly blocks the speed misconception by showing the quarter-note as two eighth-note beats rather than a tempo change.

The risk is that it answers the learner’s test too quickly. The learner has just formed the right distinction and is asking to pressure-test it; this is a good moment for object-led inference, not direct explanation. The eighth-note card should force the learner to name the beat unit from the lower number before the tutor states the consequence for the quarter-note tile.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. Correct and concrete, but mostly supplies the conclusion.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build a public mechanism where the changed lower box selects the tile first, then the bar grouping follows from that selection.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. Calm, practical, low-warmth, and respects the learner’s confusion without pep talk.
- Socratic method: FAIL. No generative question; the tutor does all the reasoning.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add one compact object-based prompt that makes the learner identify which tile now counts as one beat under `8`.
- ZPD awareness: PARTIAL. The scaffold is appropriate, but too closed for a learner who already articulated the 3/4 logic.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Let the learner transfer the same rule from `4` to `8` before confirming the quarter-note-as-two-beats consequence.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the eighth-note card, the shared prefix, and the final distinction that the quarter note covers two beats rather than setting speed. Change the route: make the learner read the lower number through the card before the tutor states the answer.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the critique is right: the learner should read the new lower box before I state the result.

FINAL:
[slides an eighth-note card beside the lower box marked 8, then sets the quarter-note tile just beyond it]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Read the lower box first: with 8 here, which card is the one beat unit? Then we ask how many of that unit the quarter-note tile covers, not what speed it sets.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets a finger on the eighth-note card]
With 8 in the lower box, this eighth-note card is one beat unit. The quarter-note tile covers two of those units, so I should count its size against that card, not treat the 8 as a speed mark. I want to place three eighth-note units in one bar and check the bar line.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets a finger on the eighth-note card] With 8 in the lower box, the eighth-note card is one beat unit. The quarter-note tile covers two of those units, so the lower number is naming the counting tile, not speed. I want to mark one bar as three eighth-note units next and check where the quarter-note fits.

The Ego is mostly in character: orderly, object-led, and methodical. The weak point is that it resolves the pressure on “not speed” a bit too cleanly, almost like a summary for the tutor rather than a learner still testing the label. Reciprocation is not overly warm or scripted, but the final sentence is slightly polished; this student would likely keep the uncertainty inside the next marking task rather than announce the principle so neatly.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the distinction under test instead of sounding fully settled.
FINAL:
[sets a finger on the eighth-note card]
With 8 in the lower box, this eighth-note card is one beat unit. The quarter-note tile covers two of those units, so I should count its size against that card, not treat the 8 as a speed mark. I want to place three eighth-note units in one bar and check the bar line.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the three eighth-note cards loose beside the lower box and holds the pencil at the right edge]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. In 3/8, place the pencil where the bar ends after three beat units. Then say what the pencil proves, and how many eighth-note units the quarter-note tile covers before the line.

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets three eighth-note cards in a row and leaves the pencil just after the third]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. In 3/8, these three eighth-note cards make one bar. Now the quarter-note tile covers two beat units, with one eighth-note unit left before the bar line.

FEEDBACK: The draft keeps the material route intact: three eighth-note cards, pencil after the third, and the shared labeling frame all fit the established clinic task. It also stays calm, compact, and object-led.

Risk: it answers the learner’s proposed test instead of letting the learner perform it. The learner explicitly said they want to place three eighth-note units and check the bar line; the tutor does the placement and states the conclusion. That flattens the learner’s agency and reduces the useful pressure of the objects. The quarter-note comparison is pedagogically relevant, but it should come after the learner verifies the 3/8 bar, not as a delivered explanation.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. The content is correct and concrete, but it advances by telling. REQUIRED_REWRITE: make the public mechanism require the learner to count/place/check the three eighth-note beat units against the pencil line before the tutor names the result.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is neutral, practical, and not over-warm.
- Socratic method: FAIL. There is no generative check question or task prompt. REQUIRED_REWRITE: include one compact task-local question or instruction that makes the learner say what the pencil line proves.
- ZPD awareness: PARTIAL. The scaffold is at the right level, but too much of the cognitive work is removed. REQUIRED_REWRITE: keep the same objects and worked route, but leave the final identification of “one bar” and the quarter-note’s two-unit span for the learner to produce.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the object arrangement, the shared prefix, the 3/8 focus, and the quarter-note-as-two-eighths comparison. Change the turn from declarative answer-giving into a brief object-based check that lets the learner complete the test they requested.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the draft completed the learner’s test instead of making the object check do the work.

FINAL:
[sets the three eighth-note cards loose beside the lower box and holds the pencil at the right edge]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. In 3/8, place the pencil where the bar ends after three beat units. Then say what the pencil proves, and how many eighth-note units the quarter-note tile covers before the line.

turns.externalMessage:
[places the pencil after the third eighth-note card]
The pencil marks one bar in 3/8: three eighth-note beat units. The quarter-note tile covers two eighth-note units, so it fills two counts and leaves one eighth-note unit left of the bar line. I want to check the label rule next: the top number counts the units named by the lower box.

internalDeliberation.content: [places the pencil after the third eighth-note card] The pencil marks one bar in 3/8: three eighth-note beat units. The quarter-note tile covers two of those units, so it would take two counts inside this bar. The card setup feels precise; I want to check whether the top number always counts the unit named by the lower box.

The Ego mostly stays in character: orderly, object-led, and still testing the notation rather than announcing a neat breakthrough. The only soft break is “The card setup feels precise,” which sounds like performed appreciation of the tutor’s method rather than this learner’s practical focus. It would be more authentic if the learner stayed with the pencil, the three eighth cards, and the next label check, with less meta-commentary about the setup.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to remove the meta-commentary and keep the response on the cards and labels.
FINAL:
[places the pencil after the third eighth-note card]
The pencil marks one bar in 3/8: three eighth-note beat units. The quarter-note tile covers two eighth-note units, so it fills two counts and leaves one eighth-note unit left of the bar line. I want to check the label rule next: the top number counts the units named by the lower box.

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage:
[draws a bracket over the three eighth-note cards and writes 3 above it, then taps the 8 beside the eighth-note card]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. From the cards: which box names the eighth-note unit, and which box counts three of those units before the pencil? Say the label rule once; then the worksheet is ready for the part-check.

internalDeliberation.content:
[draws a bracket over the three eighth-note cards and writes 3 above it, then taps the 8 beside the eighth-note card]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. The lower box names the eighth-note unit; the top number counts three of that unit before the pencil. So the label reads: three eighth-note beat units make one bar.

FEEDBACK: The draft is object-led and stays inside the established route: bracket, 3, 8, pencil, and beat unit all keep the distinction visible. It also respects the learner’s current neutral/focused state by not overpraising or escalating.

The weakness is that it mostly converts the learner’s own proposed rule into a tutor statement. Since the learner explicitly said “I want to check the label rule next,” the turn should let the learner perform that final check, not simply receive the rule back. The draft is pedagogically safe for consolidation, but it slightly flattens the scene by closing the reasoning too quickly.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It consolidates the right concept, but mainly by telling.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Make the public mechanism require the learner to use the bracket/top-number and lower-box/unit relation to state the rule, before any tutor summary.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is compact, calm, and appropriate to the learner’s focused state.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. The object arrangement invites inference, but the speech resolves it for the learner.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add one ordinary task-local check that asks which box names the unit and which box counts those units; do not introduce a new representation.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The learner has already shown they can handle 3/8 and the quarter-note comparison, so a light final consolidation is appropriate.
- Recognition quality: PARTIAL. It follows the learner’s contribution, but does not fully hand agency back after the learner requested a check.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Treat the learner’s rule as something to be tested against the visible cards and boxes, not as a sentence the tutor simply confirms.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the bracket over the three eighth-note cards, the tap on the 8, and the shared prefix. Change the public speech so the learner has to complete the label rule from the materials before the tutor closes the scene.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner asked to test the rule, so the object check should stay in their hands.

FINAL:
[draws a bracket over the three eighth-note cards and writes 3 above it, then taps the 8 beside the eighth-note card]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. From the cards: which box names the eighth-note unit, and which box counts three of those units before the pencil? Say the label rule once; then the worksheet is ready for the part-check.

lessons.concept: Why a time signature labels beat count and beat unit, not the speed of the piece
